old_claim.note: The Vindonissa field infirmary display includes herbs and ointments alongside instruments and prescriptions, so prepared medicinal materials are part of the stocked Roman medical-supply setting.

new_claim_absent: No Abydos herbal-wine dependency remains because evidence that Roman stores held herbs and ointments does not identify the scoped Predynastic fermented preparation as their prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: The Abydos study identifies herbs and resins in grape wine around 3150 BCE but does not connect that recipe to Vindonissa or Roman military provisioning.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The prerequisite node now denotes one Egyptian formulation rather than the broad class of every prepared medicinal material.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A museum display listing Roman herbs supports stocked remedies locally, but it cannot launder all such supplies through an unrelated source-pinned recipe node.

ontology_before: A broad label made any stocked herb or ointment appear to descend from the same generic preparation node.
ontology_after: Roman supply stores remain directly grounded in military medical provisioning without a false Abydos recipe lineage.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: state_medical_supply_stores no longer directly depends on herbal_medicine_preparation.
would_reject_if: Residue, text, or transmission evidence directly tied Roman military medical stores to the scoped Abydos herbal-wine process.
short_reason: Roman stocked remedies are not evidence of dependency on the Abydos recipe.
